content:
Nodejs

Rate Limit

Backend Development

Typescript

JavaScript

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI has made building software ridiculously fast.
Today, you can ask Cursor, Claude Code, ChatGPT, or GitHub Copilot to generate an Express backend, authentication system, CRUD APIs, JWT authentication, database models, and even deployment configurations — in minutes.
But while everyone is shipping faster…
There’s one security feature I keep seeing people forget.
Rate limiting.

The Problem
Imagine you build an API like this:
POST /login
POST /signup
POST /forgot-password
POST /ai/generateEverything works perfectly.
Until someone writes a simple script:
while (true) {
fetch("/api/login", {
method: "POST",
body: ...
});
}Or worse…
They spam your AI endpoint thousands of times.
Now you’ve got:

• 🚨 Higher AI bills

• 🚨 Server overload

• 🚨 Brute-force login attempts

• 🚨 Spam requests

• 🚨 Poor experience for legitimate users
The scary part?
Most AI-generated starter projects don’t include rate limiting by default.

Security Isn’t Just Authentication
Many developers think:
“I’ve added JWT authentication, so I’m secure.”
Not really.
Authentication tells you who the user is.
Rate limiting controls how much they can use your API.
They’re solving completely different problems.
A public endpoint without rate limiting is like leaving your front door unlocked, not because anyone can walk in, but because anyone can keep knocking forever.

That’s Why I Built req-guard-lite
I wanted something that was:

• Simple

• Lightweight

• TypeScript-first

• Easy to understand

• Easy to plug into any Express app
No complicated setup.
No unnecessary dependencies.
Just install it and protect your API.
npm install req-guard-lite expressThen:
import { rateLimit } from "req-guard-lite";app.use(
rateLimit({
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000,
max: 100
})
);
That’s it.
Your Express app now blocks clients that exceed your configured request limit.

Built for Small Apps…
By default, req-guard-lite uses an in-memory store.
That means:

• No Redis

• No database

• No configuration

• No extra runtime dependencies
Perfect for:

• Personal projects

• MVPs

• Internal tools

• Side projects

• Single-server deployments
Just install and go.

…But Ready to Scale
Eventually, one server isn’t enough.
Maybe you deploy:

• AWS

• Docker

• Kubernetes

• Railway

• Render

• Fly.io
Now multiple servers are handling requests.
If each server keeps its own request count, users can bypass limits simply by hitting different instances.
That’s why req-guard-lite also supports Redis.
import Redis from "ioredis";
import { createRedisStore } from "req-guard-lite/redis";const redis = new Redis();
const limiter = rateLimit({
max: 100,
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000,
store: createRedisStore(redis, {
max: 100,
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000
})
});
Now every server shares the same counters.

Not Every API Should Rate Limit by IP
Sometimes IP addresses aren’t enough.
Maybe you want limits per:

• API Key

• User ID

• Organization

• Tenant

• Subscription Plan
That’s why the middleware supports custom key generators.
const limiter = rateLimit({
max: 100,
keyGenerator: (req) =>
req.headers["x-api-key"] as string
});Or by authenticated user:
const limiter = rateLimit({
max: 50,
keyGenerator: (req) =>
(req as any).user.id
});
Bring Your Own Storage
You don’t want to use Redis.
Redis isn’t the only option.
The package exposes a simple store interface, so you can build your own backend using:

• MongoDB

• PostgreSQL

• DynamoDB

• Memcached

• SQLite

• Anything else
If you can count requests, you can plug it into req-guard-lite.

One Thing Many Developers Miss in Production
If your app sits behind:

• Nginx

• AWS Load Balancer

• Cloudflare

• Heroku
Don’t forget:
app.set("trust proxy", 1);Without it, Express may think every request comes from the proxy instead of the actual client, causing everyone to share the same rate limit.
It’s a tiny configuration that prevents a surprisingly common production issue.

Why Open Source?
I didn’t build this because existing libraries are bad.
There are already fantastic rate limiters in the Node.js ecosystem.
I built req-guard-lite because I wanted a package that I completely understood, could extend easily, and could use across my own projects. Along the way, it became a great opportunity to learn about middleware design, package publishing, testing, Redis, and building APIs that other developers can use.
If it helps someone ship a more secure Express app, that’s a bonus.

What’s Next?
The roadmap includes:

• Standard X-RateLimit-* response headers

• Better error hooks for logging and monitoring

• More extensibility while keeping the package lightweight

Final Thoughts
AI has made building software faster than ever.
But AI doesn’t automatically make software production-ready.
Security, rate limiting, validation, logging, monitoring, and deployment still require engineering decisions.
So if you’re using AI to scaffold your next Express application, take one extra minute before you deploy:
Add rate limiting.
Your servers, and your future self, will thank you.
